State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

May 10, 1983

Edward T. Clifford
Terry Appling
Greve, Clifford, Diepenbrock
and Paras
1000 G Street, Suite 400
Sacramento, CA 95814-0885

Re: Our Advice No. A-83-103

Dear Mr. Clifford and Ms. Appling:

Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of the
Paradise Irrigation District (hereinafter "District"). The
facts, as presented to me, are that the District is going to
improve its water filtration system. General plans were submitted by, among others, Frank Clendenen, the president and
principal shareholder of Clendenen Engineers. The system he
proposed is a "Modular Filtration System," which is manufactured
by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. Mr. Clendenen is the president and
100% shareholder of this company. The District approved
Mr. Clendenen's proposal and he has been hired to act as the
general engineer for the construction of the system. Your
question is whether Mr. Clendenen will have a conflict of
interest in completing the contract because of his financial
interest in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc.

DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 87100l/ prohibits a local government official from making, or participating in the making of, a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest. An official has a financial interest
in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on:

_______________________

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

Edward T. Clifford
Terry Appling
May 10, 1983
Page 2


     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more
than $1,000.

                          * * *

     (c)  Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or
more in value provided to ... the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is ... [an] officer....

     For purposes of the Political Reform Act, the term "public
official" includes consultants of local government agencies. (2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a).)  A "consultant" is a natural
person who provides, under contract, information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to a local government agency. (2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2).)  This term does not include a
person who:

     (A)  Conducts research and arrives at conclusions
with respect to his or her rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or any agency
official, other than normal contract monitoring; and

     (B)  Possesses no authority with respect to any
agency decision beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel.

                          2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                          18700(a)(2)(A) and (B).

     When an engineer is hired under a contract to perform
engineering services for a specific project, he ordinarily comes
within this exemption.  Under such circumstances, the engineer uses
his own expertise to render professional services according to the
specifications of a contract, and his judgment is not subject to
day-to-day review or direction by the District.  Essentially the
engineer is being called upon to deliver a finished project and not

Edward T. Cliford
Terry Appling
May 10, 1983
Page 3


to participate in or advise the District on general governmental
decisions requiring engineering expertise.2/

     In the present case, under the facts you have provided,
Mr. Clendenen is not a consultant under the Act. He has been
hired to construct a "Modular Filtration System" and he will
complete the project according to the specifications of the
contract. If there are deviations from the contract, such as
substitutions, these decisions will ultimately be decided by the
District Board, not Mr. Clendenen. Because Mr. Clendenen is not
a consultant, he is not subject to the conflict of interests
provisions of the Act.

     However, in the future, if the District hires an engineer to
work on other than a particular project, or provides an engineer
with more authority than Mr. Clendenen possesses in the present
situation, the District should contact us for additional advice
as to whether these engineers are consultants within the meaning
of the Act.

     I hope I have answered all of your questions. If I can be
of any additional help, please feel free to contact me at
916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

JSM:km


_______________________

     2/ This would be true even if some incidental advice
about the specific project were provided as part of these
professional services. For example, an engineer can advise a
District on what materials should be used for construction, or
that some project could not be constructed within the proposed
budget.

CLAIRE H. GREVE
EDWARD T. CLIFFORD
ANTHONY C. DIEPENBROCK
GEORGE E. PARAS
GERALD A. ROSE
VICTOR F. STEFAN
LAWRENCE A. WENCEL
GORDON W. COOK
WILLIAM C. BARRY
THOMAS S. KNOX
GARY SCOTT DECKER
TERRY A. APPLING
QUINN D. DIMAND
DENNIS W. RICHARDSON
LOUIS J. ANAPOLSKY
GARY L. VINSON
DOUGLAS L. YOUMANS
ANDREW D. WOLL
STEFAN L. MANOLAKAS
BRADLEY R. LARSON

LAW OFFICES OF

GREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK & PARAS
8450 G STREET, SUITE 400
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814-0885
(916) 443-2011

OF COUNSEL
IRVING H. PHILLIPS

GERALD R. JOHNSON (1897-1973)

1750 MONTGOMERY STREET
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111
(415) 433-0270

REPLY TO SACRAMENTO

TELECOPIER
(916) 441-7457

April 7, 1983

Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq.
Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re:  Proposed Agreement between Paradise Irrigation
     District and Clendenen Engineers

Dear Ms. Milman:

     This office represents Clendenen Engineers with
respect to a proposed contract for the design and construction
supervision of a water treatment plant for the Paradise
Irrigation District.  In the course of its review of the pro-
posed agreement in this matter, counsel for the District raised
the possibility that a potential for a conflict of interest
might exist with respect to Mr. Clendenen's interest in the
company manufacturing the filtration system which has been
selected by the District for use in its water treatment facility.
Counsel for the District later prepared an opinion to the effect
that, in their opinion, no conflict of interest exists. A copy
of their opinion is enclosed for your review.

     In the course of a telephone conversation with Ms.
Janice McLean, of your office, we were informed that the FPPC
requests the following information in assessing whether or not
a conflict of interest exists within the meaning of the Fair
Political Practices Act:

     1.  The relationship of Clendenen Engineers
to the Paradise Irrigation District (PID), and the ser-
vices they are to perform, to wit, whether or not Mr.

GREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK & PARAS

Barbara Milman, Esq.
April 7, 1983
Page two

     Clendenen is a consultant and thereby a public employee
within the meaning of the Act;

     2.  The interest of Clendenen Engineers in
Sierra Tech Systems, the patent holder and manufacturer
of the filtration system;

     3.  The decision to be made by Clendenen
Engineers pursuant to its agreement with the PID;

     4.  The role of the PID in the design of the
water treatment system;

     5.  The financial impact of the PID/Clendenen
agreement upon Sierra Tech Systems, Inc.; and,

     6.  The financial status of Sierra Tech
Systems, Inc., and whether or not the implementation of
the proposed system will confer any special benefit on
said corporation.

     This information is outlined for you below.  Mr.
Frank Clendenen is the President and principal shareholder of
Clendenen Engineers, a California corporation.  Mr. Clendenen is
also the president and 100% shareholder of Sierra Tech Systems,
Inc., the patent holder and manufacturer of MODULAR FILTER SYSTEM.

     In the summer of 1982, the PID expressed an interest
in improving its water filtration system.  This intent was acted
upon by Clendenen Engineers as well as several engineering firms,
who submitted general plans for improvements of the PID's filtra-
tion system.  On September 1, 1982, the Board of the Irrigation
District invited Clendenen Engineers, CH2M Hill of Redding, and
CDA Engineering Survey and Planning to submit filtration plan
concepts to the Board at its next meeting on September 15, 1982.
Clendenen Engineers accepted this invitation (see attached).

     At the meeting on September 15, 1982, each firm submitted
its proposals.  That submitted by Clendenen Engineers was for the
MODULAR FILTER SYSTEM.  At this time, Mr. Clendenen informed the
Board that he was the President and principal shareholder in Sierra
Tech Systems, the manufacturer of the modular filter system.  CH2M
Hill of Redding and CDA Engineering submitted plans which did not
include the modular filter system.  On the basis of the information
submitted to the Board at the September 15, 1982, meeting, it decided
to retain Clendenen Engineers to prepare a pre-design report for
the Paradise Irrigation District Water Treatment Plant.

GREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK & PARAS

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1983
Page three

Section 87.100 of the Political Reform Act provides
that:

"No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate in
making, or in any way attempt to use an official
position to influence a governmental decision in
which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest."

As you are aware, a willful violation of this provi-
sion is a misdemeanor, and any decision in violation of this
section may be enjoined. (GC §91000(b)).

In the common meaning of the word, neither Mr. Clendenen
nor Clendenen Engineers is a "public official" within the meaning
of the Act. However, GC §82048 states that:

"'[P]ublic official' means every member,
officer, and employee or consultant of a state
or local government agency."

It is clear under California law that neither Mr.
Clendenen nor Clendenen Engineers is a consultant within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act.

California Administrative Code Section 18700 defines
a "consultant" as follows:

"'[C]onsultant' shall include any natural
person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, recommendation, or counsel to a state or
local government agency, provided, however, that
'consultant' shall not include a person who '(a)
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to his or her rendition of information,
advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of
the control and direction of the agency or of any
agency official other than normal contract monitor-
ing; and (b) possesses no authority with respect to
agency decision beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation, or counsel.'"

In light of this definition, Clendenen Engineers is
not a consultant within the meaning of the Political Reform Act
for three reasons:

1. Clendenen Engineers is a corporation --
a legal individual, but not a natural person as defined
by the California Administrative Code;

GREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK & PARAS

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1982
Page four

2. At the request of the PID, Clendenen
Engineers presented its information, advice, recommendation, and counsel, independent of the control
and direction of the Agency. Therefore, it is not
a consultant with the meaning of the statute; and,

3. Clendenen possesses no authority with
respect to any Agency decision beyond its presentation of its recommendations concerning the water
treatment plant.

Clendenen Engineers was requested by the PID to make
a proposal for the construction of a water filtration plant. It
did so, with Mr. Clendenen fully explaining his involvement in
the proposed modular filter system manufactured by Sierra Tech
Systems, Inc. This was a presentation made to the Board which
was accepted. It was not a procedure whereby Mr. Clendenen or
Clendenen Engineers was sought as a consultant for the preparation of an unspecified water treatment plant. No provision of the
Political Reform Act supports a finding that Clendenen Engineers
or Mr. Frank Clendenen is a consultant subject to a potential
conflict of interest.

Assuming that neither Clendenen Engineers nor Mr. Frank
Clendenen is a consultant within the meaning of the statute, no
conflict of interest exists. Under California Administrative Code
section 18702(c) a material financial effect and, therefore, a
conflict of interest does not exist where modification, perpetuation, or renewal of a contractual relationship or opportunity
to bid competitively is the result of the consultation. At the
time Mr. Clendenen's proposal was accepted by the Irrigation
District, it made its determination to employ the Modular Filtration System. Therefore, the retention of Clendenen Engineers for
the completed design of the water treatment plant is only perpetuation of the proposal which was accepted by the Paradise Irrigation
District. The governmental decision which is crucial to the enforcement of the Political Reform Act was made prior to the retention of Clendenen Engineers by the District. Neither Mr. Clendenen
nor his firm had any input in the making of a governmental decision.

At present, Clendenen Engineers and the PID wish to
enter into an agreement for the design of the filtration plant
system. The District has already determined that it will to
implement the Modular Filtration System. Therefore, neither Mr.
Clendenen nor his firm had any input or influence as to whether
the system should be used -- the decision has already been made.
Mr. Clendenen fully disclosed his involvement in the invention
of the modular filter system at the time he made his original
presentation to the Board. The incorporation of the system into
the plans and specifications called for by the proposed agreement
does not create any conflict of interest. This decision was made

GREVE, CLIFFORD, DIEPENBROCK & PARAS

Barbara Milman
April 7, 1983
Page five

long before Mr. Clendenen engaged in activity which could possibly
be construed as "consulting" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act.

With respect to the modular filter system itself,
Government Code Section 4380 provides that the contractor awarded
the construction contract for the PID water treatment plant may
submit a request for the substitution of the system. As this
request for substitution would be evaluated and voted upon by the
Irrigation District Board rather than Clendenen Engineers, no undue
influence would exist in this respect.

While we are confident in our analysis that no conflict
exists within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, we are
cognizant of the fact that the Commission requests substantiating
data as to the interest of Mr. Clendenen in Sierra Tech Systems,
Inc. As noted above, Mr. Clendenen is the President and sole
shareholder of that firm. Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. was incorporated on June 30, 1980. Due to the youth of the corporation,
a clear financial picture is not possible. While annual sales in
fiscal 1981-1982 were approximately $396,000, these were significantly offset by developing costs. The sale of the modular filter
system to the PID would lead to a material income for the corporation. However, the system has been previously sold and tested,
and its use by the Irrigation District would not lead to any substantial profits in excess of the basic sale amount.

We trust that this correspondence sets forth all of the
information necessary for the Fair Political Practices Commission
to render its opinion. Your consideration of this matter is
greatly appreciated. We will be more than happy to answer any
further questions necessary for a resolution of this matter.

Very truly yours,

EDWARD T. CLIFFORD

ETC:lbc

MINASIAN, MINASIAN, MINASIAN, SPRUANCE, BABER & MEITH
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

DAVID H. MINASIAN, INC [illegible] (A PARTNERSHIP COMPOSED OF PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS) JOHN CARLETON GRAY, 1872-1913
PAUL R. MINASIAN, INC [illegible] 3681 BIRD STREET AT OAK STREET CARLETON GRAY, 1882-1944
WILLIAM H. SPRUANCE, INC PAUL J. JACKSON MINASIAN, 1933-1983
WILLIAM H. BABER III, INC P.O. BOX 1679
JEFFREY A. MEITH OROVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95965-1679

M. ANTHONY SOARES TELEPHONE (916) 533-2885


March 2, 1983


Board of Directors
Paradise Irrigation District
P.O. Box 128
Paradise, California 95969

Re: Clendenen Conflict of Interest

Gentlemen:

It is my understanding that in or about November
of 1982, Mr. Frank Clendenen made a presentation to the
Board of Directors of the Paradise Irrigation District,
during which he offered his engineering services and proposed
that the Board undertake the construction of a modular
filtration system. The proposed modular filtration system,
or parts or equipment included therein, is apparently
patented, with the patent being held by an entity in which
Mr. Clendenen is an officer or from which he receives income.
I am informed that this financial interest was disclosed to
the Board at the outset by Mr. Clendenen.

The Board apparently accepted Mr. Clendenen's
proposal and authorized him to prepare a Pre-Design Report
describing, among other things, a 6MGD modular treatment
plant. The Pre-Design Report has been completed and sub-
mitted to the Board. There is now being prepared an agreement
between the District and Mr. Clendenen under which the
latter party will, among other things, perform the following
work relating to the construction of the proposed modular
treatment plant:

1. Prepare final plans, specifications, and
costs estimates. Assist in securing and
analyzing bids and make recommendation for
award. Assist in preparation of contract
documents.

2. Provide contract administration of project
construction to observe compliance with
plans, specifications, and all other contract
documents. (See Proposed Agreement, Exhibit "B".)

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Two

The question has been asked: Does Mr. Clendenen,
under the circumstances set forth above, have a conflict of
interest. Although this question does not lend itself to an
absolute and unequivocal answer, it is our opinion that Mr.
Clendenen does not generally have a conflict of interest.
However, because a reasonable argument for a contrary conclusion
can be made and because there exists a substantial likelihood
that any agreement with Mr. Clendenen would be challenged on
this basis, we recommend that Mr. Clendenen be alleyed to
both the problem and the procedures set forth in Section E
of the Paradise Irrigation District Conflict of Interest
Code pursuant to which he may request from the Fair Political
Practices Commission (the administrative agency charged with
the enforcement and implementation of the Political Reform
Act) an opinion as to whether a violation exists. (Attached
is a copy of Section E.)

Since 1974, the primary source of conflict of
interest law has been the Political Reform Act, Government
Code Section 81000, et seq. The general prohibition found
in Section 87100 provides as follows:

"No public official at any level of
state or local government shall make,
participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use an official position to
influence a governmental decision in
which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest." (Emphasis
added)

A knowing and willful violation of this provision is a
misdemeanor, and the tainted governmental decision may be
enjoined. (Government Code Sections 91000, 91003(b).)

The term public official is defined in Section
82048 as follows:

"'Public official' means every member,
officer and employee or consultant of a
state or local government agency."
(Emphasis added)

The term consultant is defined in Section 18700 of
Title 2 of the California Administrative Code as follows:

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Three

"'Consultant' shall include any natural
person who provides, under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or
counsel to a state or local government
agency, provided, however, that 'consultant'
shall not include a person who: (A)
Conducts research and arrives at conclusions
with respect to his or her rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or
counsel independent of the control and
direction of the agency or of any agency
official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and (B) Possesses no authority
with respect to any agency decision
beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel."

Thus, Mr. Clendenen is a consultant within the
meaning of the Political Reform Act and its implementing
regulations if he provides information, advice, recommendation
or counsel to the Board, under contract, unless his research
and opinions are conducted and formed independently and he
possesses no authority with respect to District decisions
beyond the provision of information, advise, recommendation,
or counsel. If Mr. Clendenen's research and opinions are
not independent or if he has additional decision making
authority, he would be, to that extent, a consultant.

Even if Mr. Clendenen is, to some extent, a con-
sultant, it does not necessarily follow that he possesses an
unlawful conflict of interest. Rather, it must then be
determined whether a governmental decision in which he is
involved would have a material financial effect on his
financial interest. Section 18702(c) of Title 2 of the
California Administrative Code defines a "material financial
effect" as it applies to a consultant:

"... the making or participation in
the making of a governmental decision by
a contract consultant or by a person
retained to provide information, advice,
recommendation or counsel has no material
financial effect on a business entity or
source of income in which such consultant
or person retained is an officer, employee,
sole proprietor or partner, if the only
financial effects of the decision are
modification, perpetuation or renewal of
the contractual or retainer agreement

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Four

and/or the opportunity to bid competitively
on a project or contract."

Thus, a material financial effect does not exist
when only modification, perpetuation or renewal of a contractual
arrangement or the opportunity to bid competitively on a
project result. However, to the extent that a governmental
decision, made or participated in by Mr. Clendenen affects
not only provisions of his engineering services, but also
his financial interest in the entity holding a patent on
particular filtration system parts or equipment, the potential
for a conflict of interest exists.

The principles reflected in the above quoted
statutes and regulations must, then, be applied to the facts
and circumstances of Mr. Clendenen's situation. Mr. Clendenen's
activities, past and future, can and should be analyzed in
three stages. The first stage concerns the initial presenta-
tion of his proposal to the Board. Mr. Clendenen was not at
that stage providing information and advice under contract.
Rather, he was simply a vendor of engineering services and a
particular filtration system. As such, he was not a consultant
or public official and was not subject to the Act. (CF. 3
FPPC Opinions 71, p.3.)

After the first stage, Mr. Clendenen was authorized
by the Board to prepare a Pre-Design Report describing the
proposed modular treatment plant. At this pre-design stage,
Mr. Clendenen was providing information and advice to the
Board. However, since his research and opinions were conducted
and formed independently and since his participation in
governmental decisions did not amount to more than the providing
of information and advice, once again, he was not a consultant
or public official and was not subject to the Act.

The third stage of activity will involve the
implementation of the modular treatment plant proposal.
Inasmuch as Mr. Clendenen may, in addition to providing
information and advice, make or participate in decisions
during this stage, the potential for conflict exists.
Although each governmental decision would need to be evaluated
in the light of Mr. Clendenen's activity of role and his
financial interest, we do not believe the mere incorporation
of the patented parts and equipment into the plans and
specifications would violate the conflict of interest
provisions. As a practical matter, this decision was made
by the Board during the first stage of Mr. Clendenen's
activity, before he became a "consultant".

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Five

     As we have indicated, there is the potential for
conflict during the third stage of activity. For example,
Government Code Section 4380 provides:

     "No agency of the state nor any political
subdivision, municipal corporation, or
district, nor any public officer or
person charged with the letting of
contracts for the construction, alteration
or repair of public works shall draft or
cause to be drafted specifications for
bids, in connection with the construction,
alteration, or repair of public works,
(1) in such a manner as to limit the
bidding, directly or indirectly, to any
one specific concern, or (2) except in
those instances where the product is
designated to match others in use on a
particular public improvement either
completed or in the course of completion,
calling for a designated material,
product, thing, or service by specific
brand or trade name unless the specification
lists at least two brands or trade names
of comparable quality or utility and is
followed by the words 'or equal' so that
bidders may furnish any equal material,
product, thing, or service. In applying
this section, the specifying agency
shall, if aware of an equal product
manufactured in California, name such
product in the specification. In those
cases involving a unique or novel product
application required to be used in the
public interest, or where only one brand
or trade name is known to the specifying
agency, it may list only one. Specifications
shall provide a period of time of at
least 35 days after award of the contract
for submission of data substantiating a
request for a substitution of 'an equal'
item."

     We have been advised, and are assuming, that the
patented parts or equipment are unique or novel items, and,
therefore, lie within the exception to the general prohibition
of Section 4380. Nonetheless, the contractor awarded the

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Six

construction contract may, by statute, submit a request for
substitution and data supporting such request. In the light
of Mr. Clendenen's interest in the entity supplying the
parts or equipment incorporated into the plans and specifi-
cations, we would recommend that he not be involved in any
decision concerning a request for substitution.

Finally, another, and arguably more restrictive,
conflict of interest law should be mentioned. Government
Code Section 1090 provides:

"Members of the Legislature, state,
county, district, judicial district, and
city officers or employees shall not be
financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity,
or by any body or board of which they
are members. Nor shall state, county,
district, judicial district, and city
officers or employees be purchasers at
any sale or vendors at any purchase made
by them in their official capacity.

As used in the Article 'district' means
any agency of the state formed pursuant
to general law or special act, for the
local performance of governmental or
proprietary functions within limited
boundaries."

The courts of this State have held that a person
merely in an advisory position to a public agency is affected
by this conflict of interest rule. (Schaefer v. Berinstein
(1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291) They have also held that
the word "made" is not used in the statute in its narrower
and technical contract sense but is used in its broad sense
to encompass such embodiment in the making of a contract as
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reason-
ing, planning, drawing of plans and specifications and
solicitations for bids. Millbrae Assn. for Residential
Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222. A
contract or transaction entered into in violation of Section
1090 is void. (Government Code Section 1092) The prohibition
found in Section 1090 has apparently survived the enactment
of the Political Reform Act. It has been stated informally
by the Attorney General's Office that such continues to

Board of Directors
March 2, 1983
Page Seven

apply in contractual situations, while all other governmental
decisions would be subject to the Political Reform Act.
However, the Political Reform Act specifically provides that
it supersedes other conflicting provisions. It is our
opinion that in the light of the detailed handling of
consultant transactions under the Political Reform Act and
the implementing regulations, the provisions of Section 1090
are superseded and compliance with the Political Reform Act
will suffice.

Notwithstanding our opinion that a conflict does
not exist, and in the light of the potential sanctions, we
direct your attention to Section E of the Paradise Irrigation
District Conflict of Interest Code, titled "Opinions of the
Commission and Counsel", which provides a procedure whereby
any official that is unsure of his duty, right, or privilege
of participation, may request from the Fair Political Practices
Commission an opinion regarding a political conflict of
interest. The Section also provides that the attorney for
the District may file with such request its proposed opinion.
We recommend that Mr. Glendenen be apprised of the potential
problem and his opinion to request an FPPC opinion clarifying
the matter under the Code.

If you have any further questions or if you other-
wise wish to discuss this matter, please do not hesitate to
contact us.

Very truly yours,

MINASIAN, MINASIAN, MINASIAN,
SPRUANCE, BABER & MEITH

By_________________________________
M. ANTHONY SOARES

MAS/krh

Enclosure

to the extent that his participation is legally required for the action
or decision to be made. The fact that an official's vote is needed to
break a tie does not make his participation legally required for the
purposes of this section. The attorney for the District shall advise
any official whether or not the "Rule of Necessity" is applicable on
a case-by-case basis. (87101; Reg 18701)

Section E. OPINIONS OF THE COMMISSION AND COUNSEL: (Note: FFPC
has not reviewed this provision.)

Any official who is unsure of any duty, right, or privilege of partici-
pation in any matter under this Code or under the provisions of the
Political Reform Act of 1974 may request the Fair Political Prac-
tices Commission to issue an opinion with respect to his duties
under Title 9 of the Government Code. At the time such request is
made, or thereafter, the attorney for the District may file with such
request a proposed opinion with respect to the official's duties. The
attorney shall state the facts upon which his opinion is based, and
all such opinions shall contain citations of the law or other authorities
and be public records of the District. If the commission has not ren-
dered an opinion with respect to the official's duties within 45 days
of such filing, then the official may rely on the opinion of counsel
until an opinion is rendered by the Commission, and if the opinion of
the attorney or the opinion of the Commission is complied with in
good faith, such official shall be exempt from criminal or civil lia-
bility and other sanctions provided for in the Government Code
provided that the material facts are as stated in the opinion request.
(Based in part upon language of Government Code, Section 83114.)

-7-

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 19, 1983

Edward T. Clifford
Greve, Clifford, Diepenbrock
& Peras
1000 G Street, Suite 400
Sacramento, CA 95814-0835

Re: A-83-103

Dear Mr. Clifford:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shenk McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Frank Clendenen, president and 100% shareholder of Sierra Tech Systems Inc., has a conflict of interest serving as general engineer for Paradise Irrigation District's water filtration system project when Sierra Tech manufactures the proposed filtration system.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Clendenen does not have a conflict of interest because he is not a consultant under the Political Reform Act—he was hired to construct a specific project according to contract specifications, not to participate in governmental decisions. However, future engagements with different scope or authority levels should be reviewed separately.

Extracted information from FPPC response portion. Clear Question and Conclusion sections present. Document discusses consultant exemption under Government Code Section 87100 and California Administrative Code Section 18700.

CONCLUSION: Because Mr. Clendenen is not a consultant, he is not subject to the conflict of interests provisions of the Act. However, in the future, if the District hires an engineer to work on other than a particular project, or provides an engineer with more authority than Mr. Clendenen possesses in the present situation, the District should contact us for additional advice as to whether these engineers are consultants within the meaning of the Act.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 May 10, 1983 Edward T. Clifford Terry Appling Greve, Clifford, Diepenbrock and Paras 1000 G Street, Suite 400 Sacramento, CA 95814-0885 Re: Our Advice No. A-83-103 Dear Mr. Clifford and Ms. Appling: Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of the Paradise Irrigation District (hereinafter "District"). The facts, as presented to me, are that the District is going to improve its water filtration system. General plans were submitted by, among others, Frank Clendenen, the president and principal shareholder of Clendenen Engineers. The system he proposed is a "Modular Filtration System," which is manufactured by Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. Mr. Clendenen is the president and 100% shareholder of this company. The District approved Mr. Clendenen's proposal and he has been hired to act as the general engineer for the construction of the system. Your question is whether Mr. Clendenen will have a conflict of interest in completing the contract because of his financial interest in Sierra Tech Systems, Inc. DISCUSSION Government Code Section 87100l/ prohibits a local government official from making, or participating in the making of, a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on: _______________________ 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Edward T. Clifford Terry Appling May 10, 1983 Page 2 (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000. * * * (c) Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or more in value provided to ... the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (d) Any business entity in which the public official is ... [an] officer.... For purposes of the Political Reform Act, the term "public official" includes consultants of local government agencies. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a).) A "consultant" is a natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a local government agency. (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2).) This term does not include a person who: (A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with respect to his or her rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the control and direction of the agency or any agency official, other than normal contract monitoring; and (B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2)(A) and (B). When an engineer is hired under a contract to perform engineering services for a specific project, he ordinarily
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter A-83-103 addresses whether Frank Clendenen has a conflict of interest when hired as general engineer for Paradise Irrigation District's water filtration system while holding financial interest in Sierra Tech Systems, the manufacturer of the proposed filtration system. The FPPC concludes no conflict exists because Clendenen is performing professional engineering services on a specific project rather than serving as a consultant making governmental decisions.
